name: Event-capture strategy: extended stays versus layered escalation
consequences: Choosing whether to routinely extend participant stays to improve event yield directly determines staffing cycles, facility throughput, and recruitment burden. Longer stays generate richer phenotyping data but reduce participant turnover, require larger compensation budgets, and create idle-suite risk if yield remains low. Shorter stays with escalation protocols improve facility utilization but risk missing low-frequency episodes and reduce statistical power for between-person patterns.
options: Enforce a standard 14-day baseline stay for all NREM participants with no extension unless adjudicated events exceed three, prioritizing facility throughput and staff predictability

Condition stay length dynamically on event yield: standard 10-day baseline with automatic 2-week extension if zero adjudicated events during week one, capping total at 10 weeks and accepting variable workload

Adopt a tiered escalation model where all participants stay 10 days minimum, but only those with zero captured events in week one receive offers to extend, paired with staggered overnight PSG deployment to concentrate monitoring resources on likely high-yield windows
review: Stay length directly competes with recruitment and budget constraints. The core tension is balancing event yield probability against operational cost and staff burnout — longer stays improve data quality but the options all assume extended stays remain feasible within the €80/night compensation and 3-year budget envelope. None explicitly addresses the threshold at which unproductive admissions (capped at 20% per protocol) become unsustainable, forcing a hard redesign of inclusion criteria or compensation strategy.

name: Sensing architecture: tiered PSG scheduling versus continuous clinical-grade monitoring
consequences: Restricting full PSG to three scheduled nights plus event-triggered nights preserves budget and reduces technician workload but may miss prodromal or post-event physiology. Continuous full-montage monitoring across all nights generates maximal clinical resolution and event context but consumes equipment, technician time, and data storage at substantially higher cost, risking sustainability if hardware failure or staffing gaps occur. Hybrid scheduling forces judgment calls on which nights warrant upgrade, introducing bias.
options: Deliver full PSG montage on all nights for all participants, accepting doubled equipment and technician burden, justified by maximum event characterization and elimination of night-selection bias

Maintain the current protocol: scheduled PSG on first, mid-stay, and final nights plus event-triggered escalation nights, supplemented by nightly dry-electrode EEG and contact-free sensors, balancing cost against clinical resolution

Randomize PSG scheduling across all nights for each participant (first night always included), ensuring unbiased temporal coverage while preserving average resource commitment, but introducing scheduling complexity and participant unpredictability around which night will be instrumented
review: The core tension is cost-driven data completeness — continuous PSG is scientifically ideal but fiscally unsustainable; scheduled PSG economizes but introduces selection bias if certain night types reliably produce more events. None of the options directly address the risk that contact-free mattress and dry-electrode EEG may fail to trigger escalation events that full PSG would have captured, creating systematic underestimation of episode frequency.

name: Data sharing and replicability: privacy-bounded repository versus restricted-access consortium
consequences: Depositing only de-identified physiological and sensor data without video in a public repository maximizes transparency and enables secondary analysis but excludes the behavioral context that would strengthen event classification and reproducibility of morphology phenotypes. Restricting all data to a closed consortium preserves privacy and institutional control but fractures the scientific record and limits independent validation of findings. Neither path fully reconciles clinical video necessity with data-use ethics.
options: Deposit all de-identified continuous EEG, EOG, EMG, mattress sensor streams, and aggregated movement metrics in a public BIDS-compatible repository without any video, accepting the limitation that morphological phenotyping remains opaque to external researchers

Establish a tiered-access data use agreement for a managed consortium including the original site, 2–3 external sleep centers, and regulatory bodies; restrict motion video to consortium sites with signed institutional agreements while sharing deidentified physiology publicly, balancing openness with privacy

Retain all data including de-identified video streams on a secure university server accessible only through written application to the PI and external advisory board, maximizing privacy and replication control at the cost of fragmenting the scientific commons and slowing secondary research
review: Privacy and replicability sit in direct tension—video is essential for event validation but ethically fraught; deidentification is incomplete for bedroom video because behavioral patterns and room layout can re-identify residents. The options assume data stewardship capacity and institutional incentive alignment are stable over a 10-year retention horizon, but neither addresses the risk that shifting privacy regulations (GDPR, national laws) may retroactively constrain archival video even if initially consented.

name: Semi-automated event-triage development: human-in-the-loop annotation versus algorithm-first discovery
consequences: Building the triage tool by training on real-time technician flags and dual-rater consensus prioritizes immediate utility and maintains human oversight but locks the algorithm to current human bias and miss-rates. Training on unsupervised clustering or weak labels from sensor patterns alone risks autonomous detection of non-parasomnia artifacts but accelerates discovery of novel event morphologies. Each path trades speed and novelty for reliability and clinician alignment.
options: Adopt a human-in-the-loop approach: collect all real-time technician annotations and dual-rater consensus labels in the first 12 months, then train the triage tool exclusively on this supervised dataset, validating performance against held-out dual-rater agreement, ensuring transparent lineage

Implement parallel unsupervised clustering of sensor and EEG features across all nights in parallel with human scoring; use unsupervised clusters to identify event subtypes and edge cases that dual-rater consensus may miss, then synthesize findings into a hybrid model that flags both learned patterns and consensus-defined events

Commission a separate contract with an external computational neuroscience group to develop the triage tool independently on site data without access to technician real-time flags, forcing algorithm discovery to rely purely on physiological signal pattern matching and inter-rater scoring, eliminating circularity but risking discovery lag
review: Event definition sits at the intersection of subjectivity and automation—AASM criteria are standardized but inter-rater reliability in the field remains imperfect, and the options risk training the algorithm on inherited human disagreement as ground truth. The core tension is whether the triage tool should optimize for speed (flagging anything vaguely event-like to reduce technician review) or specificity (catching only high-confidence events, accepting some miss); none of the options explicitly parameterize this trade-off or plan for iteration if unsupervised approaches discover that technician real-time flags are systematically uncalibrated.

name: Facility expansion: physical capacity build-out versus distributed network replication
consequences: Expanding in Bonn from 8 to 12 active suites concentrates operational expertise and simplifies protocol fidelity but commits capital to a single site, reduces flexibility if recruitment plateaus, and increases risk if staffing or safety issues force temporary closure. Replicating the residential unit at partner sites (e.g., other German sleep centers) distributes geographic recruitment, accelerates participant enrollment, and hedges operational risk but introduces protocol drift, requires distributed training, and fragments data pipelines. Early proof-of-concept limits expansion but prolongs single-site bottleneck.
options: Expand the Bonn facility to 12 active suites by month 18 if pilot data confirm safe operations and 70%+ event-yield rates, reinvesting cost savings from years one and two, accepting geographic concentration risk

Defer on-site expansion and instead negotiate mirror-site agreements with two regional sleep centers (Cologne, Hanover) to replicate the protocol with centralized technician training, dual-scoring oversight from Bonn, and federated data pipelines, spreading recruitment and distributing operational burden

Execute a phased build-out: expand Bonn to 10 suites by month 15 if pilot success metrics are met, then freeze expansion and evaluate external replication based on interim publication impact and funding prospects, preserving capital optionality
review: Expansion timing is coupled tightly to pilot success metrics (event yield, safety, annotation throughput), but the protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions creates a hard boundary that may trigger before operational maturity is proven. The core tension is whether to consolidate operational learning in a single site (ensuring protocol fidelity) or parallelize enrollment across distributed sites (risking quality drift). None of the options pre-emptively address the training and quality-assurance cost of replication, which could consume budget originally allocated to year-two participant enrollment.

name: Recruitment gatekeeping: clinical pre-screening stringency versus inclusive entry criteria
consequences: Strict pre-admission screening (specialist interview, bed partner collateral, prior PSG, exclusion checklist) ensures enrolled participants meet phenotypic inclusion criteria and reduces false admissions but narrows the eligible pool, increases referral-to-enrollment friction, and biases recruitment toward patients already embedded in tertiary care. Relaxed screening with assessment deferred to on-site evaluation broadens participation and reduces referral barriers but increases the risk of admitting mimics (nocturnal epilepsy, sleep-related hypoventilation) and drives up protocol-violation rates. The balance determines recruitment velocity and phenotypic purity.
options: Require all prospective participants to undergo a certified sleep specialist interview and prior polysomnography before admission, using a detailed exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI, accepting reduced enrollment velocity in exchange for high phenotypic confidence and zero-tolerance safety risk

Adopt a two-stage screen: brief telephone intake by study coordinator to rule out absolute contraindications (active substance use, severe psychiatric instability), then complete clinical assessment on-site in the first 24 hours, permitting provisional enrollment before full adjudication, accepting higher screen-fail rates but accelerating recruitment

Collaborate with the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin to bundle recruitment with a parallel observational screening study in sleep clinics; enroll participants who meet provisional criteria with confirmatory imaging or EEG available post-hoc, shifting gate-keeping from pre-admission to post-hoc phenotypic validation, trading upfront friction for downstream exclusion complexity
review: Pre-admission screening directly affects recruitment timeline and budget (study coordinator effort, outreach throughput), but the inclusion criterion of 'at least 2 self-reported episodes per month' is self-reported and unverified until on-site monitoring begins. The core tension is diagnostic confidence at enrollment versus recruitment speed. None of the options address the risk that participants who report high frequency at intake may have much lower in-facility yield due to adaptation, regression to the mean, or environment-dependency of parasomnia expression—creating a feedback loop where strict screening reduces admissions of already-rare phenotypes, compounding recruitment lag.

name: Funding pathway: academic-only model versus pharmaceutical partnership framing
consequences: Depending solely on DFG and University of Bonn funding maintains editorial independence and publication timeline but limits total budget flexibility and may require cost-cutting (delayed expansion, reduced staff, shorter stays) if funding contingencies trigger. Securing a pharmaceutical industry partner upside (30-day pre-publication comment window, no equity or editorial control per protocol) unlocks capital and leverages commercial interest in parasomnia biology but introduces perceived bias risk, complicates publication strategy (timing, journal selection), and creates reputational dependency on partner continuity. Rejection of pharma funding preserves pure academic standing but may constrain scope and extend timeline.
options: Maintain the academic-only funding model; focus DFG and internal funding strategy on demonstrating proof-of-concept in years one and two, then position year-three contingency funding on published results and external validation, accepting longer timelines and potential scope reduction if contingencies are denied

Actively pursue a pharmaceutical partnership in the first six months (neurology or sleep pharma company interested in parasomnia biology), structure the agreement to preserve publication rights with a 30-day comment window, and use partner funding to unlock immediate expansion to 12 suites and extended participant compensation to €100/night, accelerating recruitment

Adopt a hybrid model: secure DFG and university funding as the primary pillars, but open exploratory discussions with pharma partners without committing to a formal agreement; if a partner emerges by month 12 with acceptable governance, negotiate entry; if not, proceed with academic funding alone, preserving optionality
review: Funding source choice is inseparable from publication strategy and reputational positioning in academic neuroscience, but the protocol bans the word 'validated' and stresses this is early-stage methodology work, not a finished diagnostic product—this framing limits pharma marketing appeal and may render industry partnerships less financially attractive than they appear. The core tension is capital-driven acceleration versus academic independence. None of the options explicitly cost the reputational risk or publication-timing friction of a pharma partnership, nor do they address whether pharmaceutical interest in parasomnia biology is durable enough to sustain a 3-year partnership if preliminary results are uninspiring.

name: Staff model: centralized night coverage versus rotating on-call backup model
consequences: Three rotating technicians providing full overnight coverage on all nights ensures immediate response to events, minimizes annotation delays, and meets safety requirements for 8 active suites, but creates high burnout risk, limits flexibility for vacation and illness, and requires continuous payroll throughout the year including off-season periods. A smaller core team (one technician per night) with on-call backup reduces baseline headcount and cost but introduces response latency, creates annotation backlog after high-event nights, and heightens safety risk if simultaneous events occur in multiple suites. Staffing model directly determines operational resilience and participant safety margins.
options: Hire three full-time research technicians working rotating night shifts (each covering ~2.7 nights per week across 8 suites), ensuring continuous on-site presence, event response, and real-time annotation with standard German employment benefits and social contributions, accepting year-round payroll cost and burnout risk

Deploy a core team of 1.5 technicians (one full-time, one 50% contract) for routine nights plus an on-call technician pool (graduate students, sleep medicine residents) at €25/hour on-call stipend and €40/hour if called in; reduces baseline headcount but introduces annotation lag and requires robust escalation protocols for high-event nights

Maintain three technicians but shift two to part-time status (70% FTE each) contingent on first 12-month event-yield data; if average yield is <1 adjudicated event per 10 participant-nights, freeze hiring at current level; if yield justifies full coverage, convert to three FTE by month 18, deferring cost commitment until operational case is demonstrated
review: Staff model is the primary determinant of operational safety and quality, but none of the options price in the hidden cost of technician retraining or turnover if burnout forces resignation mid-project—replacing a trained technician requires 2–3 months of ramp-up and disrupts continuity. The core tension is continuous oversight (safety, quality) versus personnel sustainability (avoiding burnout and cost overrun). The options assume German employment law protections and wage expectations remain stable over three years, which may underestimate pressure to raise technician compensation if recruitment for overnight sleep research proves difficult.

name: Publication and dissemination: closed-protocol results versus open-science transparency cadence
consequences: Publishing a comprehensive methods paper and longitudinal phenotyping study at the end of year three maximizes sample consolidation and statistical power but delays open dissemination, risks being scooped by faster-moving groups, and reduces real-time feedback from the field. Publishing methods and preliminary results incrementally (months 12, 18, 24) accelerates community review and shapes protocol refinement but fragments the narrative, requires provisional analysis and conditional conclusions, and may invite criticism of incomplete data. Timing strategy affects both scientific impact and the phase-two funding landscape.
options: Adopt a delayed-publication strategy: complete the full three-year cohort and dual-scoring, then submit 2–3 integrated papers in year three to high-impact sleep medicine journals, maximizing sample size and methodological maturity, accepting publication lag and information embargo until year four

Execute a rolling-publication model: submit a methods paper by month 12 describing the residential monitoring protocol and semi-automated triage validation, preliminary phenotyping results from year one at month 18, and longitudinal characterization from the full cohort at month 36, enabling real-time field feedback and earlier phase-two grant positioning

Establish a public project dashboard updated quarterly with de-identified enrollment metrics, event-yield summaries, and algorithm performance updates, paired with a final comprehensive methods paper at month 36; maximize transparency and reproducibility signal to funders and the community, but accept stripped-down early reporting that defers deep interpretation
review: Publication timing is decoupled from scientific rigor—early papers can be rigorous but preliminary; delayed papers can suffer from field obsolescence or scooping. The core tension is information asymmetry (delayed sharing hoards data until perfection) versus reputational benefit of leadership in open-science practices. None of the options address the risk that preliminary publications may lock interpretations that later data contradicts, forcing retraction or clarification that damages credibility, nor do they anticipate whether high-impact journals will accept a methods paper without statistically powered clinical findings.

name: Inpatient event-yield optimization: extended-stay protocols versus targeted-slot scheduling
consequences: Longer participant stays increase the probability of capturing rare parasomnia episodes but lock capital in fewer concurrent admissions and accumulate annotation backlog; shorter, more frequent slot rotations maximize throughput and reduce per-participant cost but risk higher unproductive admission rates and dilute longitudinal within-person pattern recognition. The choice directly affects recruitment messaging, facility occupancy rates, and whether stays remain adaptive versus standardized.
options: Establish a flexible stay model with initial 2-week baseline, automatic extension to 4 weeks for any participant with zero captured events in the first fortnight, and conditional 2-week extensions only after event capture with strong evidence of clustering, capping total stay at 10 weeks to balance longitudinal depth against facility turnover.

Adopt fixed 3-week residential blocks regardless of event yield, with no extensions offered, accepting that approximately 20–25% of admissions will be unproductive but enabling predictable cohort rotations, faster data throughput, and simplified scheduling logic.

Implement a slot-reservation system where participants book 5-day intensive baseline stays, then return for 3-day booster blocks at months 1, 3, and 6 post-discharge if events were captured; this distributed model reduces housing costs but requires commute feasibility and tolerates fragmented longitudinal data collection.
review: The core tension is between statistical power (longer stays yield more events) and economic efficiency (shorter stays reduce per-participant cost). None of the options address what happens to participants whose events cluster in the first week: under the first approach they leave after 2 weeks with rich data but miss potential late-stage patterns; under the second they stay the full 3 weeks with idle nights; under the third, booster scheduling is opportunistic and may miss sustained phenotyping.

name: Technician-first annotation burden: real-time single-review flagging versus asynchronous dual-review staging
consequences: Real-time technician flagging during the night accelerates escalation-night PSG deployment and reduces annotation latency but risks alert fatigue, inconsistent criterion application, and sleep-deprived judgment errors under 8-hour shifts; asynchronous dual-review defers event-triage rigor to daytime raters but delays escalation nights and requires technicians to passively log continuous sensor streams. This choice cascades into staffing skill requirements, algorithm handoff timing, and data-quality variability across the study cohort.
options: Train night technicians as AASM-criterion experts with real-time annotation authority, using a structured decision tree to flag probable parasomnia events during the night and trigger same-night PSG escalation when high-confidence criteria are met, accepting occasional false-positive escalations in exchange for immediate clinical-grade capture opportunities.

Assign technicians a passive monitoring role focused on safety and equipment function only, storing all flagged-sensor signals for batch annotation by two independent raters the next morning; escalation nights are then scheduled reactively for nights with consensus-flagged events, reducing technician cognitive load but delaying capture by approximately 24 hours.

Implement a hybrid model where technicians use a simple probabilistic sensor checklist (rapid movement amplitude, arousal pattern, respiratory transience) to queue signals for immediate algorithmic ranking; the algorithm suggests top-confidence candidates within 30 minutes; technicians confirm or override the algorithm's suggestion and deploy escalation PSG, combining human judgment with computational speed.
review: The core tension is responsiveness versus accuracy: real-time technician flagging can miss subtle events or flag artifacts as events due to fatigue and limited visual cues; asynchronous dual-review is more rigorous but sacrifices the opportunity to capture recurrent events on the same night. The hybrid approach introduces algorithmic black-box decisions into a high-stakes gate, which could bias event detection toward sensor-detectable morphologies and away from rare or atypical presentations.

name: Matched-control cohort design: concurrent parallel recruitment versus sequential enrollments
consequences: Concurrent recruitment of age- and sex-matched controls during the pilot year allows immediate head-to-head sensor-baseline comparison and accelerates event-triage algorithm validation but complicates facility scheduling and divides staff attention between patient and control protocols; sequential enrollment of controls after completion of the NREM parasomnia cohort simplifies logistics but delays control data acquisition and creates temporal mismatches in environmental factors (seasonal variations, equipment firmware updates). The choice affects statistical power and publication timeline for the benchmarking aim.
options: Designate 2 of the 8 active suites as control-dedicated slots and enroll a matched control participant immediately after each NREM parasomnia admission; this ensures contemporaneous sensor streams, uniform facility conditions, and immediate algorithmic comparison, but requires fine-grained scheduling to pair age and sex.

Recruit all 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants first during months 1–24, then enroll 20–25 age- and sex-matched controls during months 24–30 in a separate protocol block, reducing scheduling friction and allowing technicians to stabilize parasomnia operations before pivoting to control protocols.

Enroll controls opportunistically throughout the study whenever a suitable matched candidate (age within 3 years, same sex, no sleep disorders) volunteers, accepting asynchronous recruitment but capturing diverse seasonal and technical conditions; this flexible approach distributes control data across the study timeline and reflects real recruitment unpredictability.
review: The core tension is operational simplicity versus statistical power: concurrent enrollment guarantees matched conditions but complicates room management; sequential enrollment is logistically clean but introduces seasonal and technical confounds (e.g., equipment firmware updated between parasomnia and control phases). None of the options address how to handle control participants who develop or disclose undiagnosed sleep disorders during the study, potentially contaminating the baseline.

name: Pre-admission screening stringency: rapid-access pathway versus neurologic gating specialist review
consequences: A streamlined telephone and questionnaire-based pre-screening accelerates enrollment and maximizes recruitment reach but risks admitting participants with subtle epilepsy mimics or occult sleep apnea that violate exclusion criteria; mandatory specialist neurologic evaluation (EEG, sleep medicine assessment) before admission ensures safety and criterion fidelity but adds 4–8 weeks to enrollment time and may deter marginal candidates from completing the pathway. This choice directly affects cohort purity, safety incident rates, and recruitment timeline feasibility.
options: Implement a 15-minute structured telephone interview guided by a checklist, supplemented by written self-report questionnaires and bed-partner collateral history, with PI adjudication of borderline cases; admission proceeds within 2 weeks of contact, prioritizing speed and reach over diagnostic certainty.

Require all candidates to undergo in-person neurologic evaluation at University Hospital Bonn including wake and sleep EEG, formal sleep medicine history, and neuropsychiatric screening by the clinical psychologist; only participants cleared by this 2-hour evaluation proceed to residential admission, adding 6–8 weeks but ensuring exclusion-criterion rigor.

Adopt a tiered approach: rapid telephone screening for obvious inclusions (clear history, confirmed prior PSG, stable household), with specialist referral mandated only for candidates with ambiguous nocturnal seizure features, untreated apnea symptoms, or psychiatric complexity; approximately 40–50% of screened candidates proceed to residential admission without specialist review.
review: The core tension is speed versus safety: rapid access maximizes enrollment numbers but risks admitting participants whose conditions mimic parasomnias; mandatory specialist review ensures safety but creates dropout risk in the funnel and delays study timeline. None of the options establish a protocol-defined escalation pathway for participants who disclose new medical information or adverse events during the residential stay that retrospectively warrant earlier exclusion.

name: Longitudinal data stewardship: live-access research repository versus embargoed archive with staged release schedule
consequences: Depositing de-identified physiological data in a public repository (e.g., OpenNeuro, PhysioNet) immediately upon study completion maximizes scientific transparency and enables rapid reuse by external researchers but sacrifices negotiating power with potential pharma collaborators and exposes raw data to reidentification risk if sensor correlations with published phenotype data are discovered; an embargoed archive with staged 12-month or 24-month release delays external access but allows the primary team to establish publication priority and conduct additional privacy-vetting analyses. This choice shapes the funding partnership model and publication strategy.
options: Deposit de-identified physiological data (EEG, sensor streams, arousal/movement annotations) to OpenNeuro within 3 months of study completion with unrestricted CC-BY license; video and behavioral metadata remain withheld and are destroyed after analysis, maximizing transparency and enabling immediate community validation.

Establish an internal institutional repository under University of Bonn governance with data access restricted to approved researchers under formal data use agreements; data is released 24 months after publication of the primary Aim 2 phenotyping manuscript, allowing the team priority publication windows and enabling pharma-sponsored reanalysis under controlled licensing terms.

Create a two-tier system: physiological waveforms and event annotations are released publicly 12 months post-completion, while sensor-derived features (movement envelope, arousal clustering indices) remain embargoed for 24 months; this balances open science with competitive advantage, allowing raw-data replication while protecting the team's derived analytic products.
review: The core tension is scientific openness versus data control: immediate public release enables reproducibility but forecloses negotiation leverage with industry partners and accelerates risk of reidentification if external researchers link sensor patterns to clinical publications. None of the options establish a formal protocol for what constitutes de-identification failure (e.g., if a published case report's temporal event pattern matches a public repository participant) or who bears liability if such linkage occurs.

name: Event-capture adequacy threshold: fixed enrollment ceiling versus adaptive milestone-triggered review gates
consequences: A predetermined enrollment target of 50–70 participants provides clear scope and predictable completion but risks completing the study with inadequate event yields if participants have lower-than-expected event frequency; adaptive review gates that require external board assessment at month 18 if enrollment lags or captured events fall below thresholds allow mid-course corrections and potential protocol modifications but introduce uncertainty, potential scope expansion, and repeat review cycles that delay progress. This choice affects grant reporting milestones, publication planning, and program continuity confidence.
options: Fix enrollment at 60 NREM parasomnia participants plus 20 matched controls regardless of event yield; if fewer than 40 independently adjudicated events are captured by month 24, extend the study by 6 months for continued recruitment at no additional budget, capping the protocol at 30 months.

Establish a hard enrollment ceiling of 50 participants; if by month 18 enrollment is below 25 or adjudicated events are below 40, commission an external review panel to recommend either stay-the-course with extended timeline, modify inclusion criteria to broaden the phenotype, or close to new enrollment and analyze available data, accepting that Aim 2 may be powered only for subgroup analysis.

Implement a two-gate model: at month 12 conduct an interim analysis; if event capture is trending toward 80% of the target, continue as planned; if below 60%, trigger protocol amendment review with options to extend stay durations, broaden recruitment channels, or add a second site; at month 18, make a final commitment decision based on observed yield curves.
review: The core tension is certainty versus adaptability: fixed enrollment guarantees known scope but may deliver underpowered analyses; adaptive gates allow course correction but introduce governance overhead and create intervals of uncertainty that complicate downstream grant funding. None of the options quantify the cost of protocol amendments or site expansion, nor do they specify how to interpret event-yield trends if stays are extended—delayed events might reflect selection bias (only persistent cases remain) rather than genuine low population frequency.

name: Semi-autonomous triage rollout: algorithm-as-secondary-filter versus integrated human-algorithm consensus gate
consequences: Using the semi-automated event-triage model as a secondary filter for technician review (technician flags, algorithm ranks confidence, human makes final call) preserves human accountability and allows for rejection of high-confidence algorithmic calls that contradict clinical judgment; integrating the algorithm into the primary decision gate (algorithm suggests escalation threshold, technician confirms or escalates to PI override only) reduces manual labor but risks anchoring bias, where technicians defer to algorithmic confidence scores even for atypical morphologies. This choice affects staffing hours, escalation-night frequency, and transparency in downstream publications about how decisions were made.
options: Deploy the algorithm as a ranking tool: technicians review flagged signals and see algorithmic confidence scores (0–100%) displayed alongside their own judgment; technicians make independent accept-or-reject calls for escalation night PSG and document their reasoning; algorithm performance is later evaluated as agreement with consensus dual-rater scoring, not as a primary diagnostic gate.

Embed the algorithm directly into the escalation decision: if the algorithm assigns a signal >80% confidence as probable parasomnia and the event-checklist sensor pattern is consistent (e.g., large movement + arousal), escalation PSG is deployed automatically unless the technician explicitly overrides within 10 minutes; this reduces technician cognitive load but requires explicit override documentation and PI spot-checks for override-pattern audits.

Implement a consensus model where the algorithm's top-confidence candidates are flagged for technician review, and escalation PSG is triggered only if both technician and algorithm independently concur (overlap in top 5 candidates within a 3-hour window); this raises the bar for escalation but ensures neither human nor algorithm makes unilateral decisions, reducing false-positive PSG deployments.
review: The core tension is efficiency versus accountability: algorithm-as-filter reduces labor but creates a two-stage gate that may discard real events if technician skepticism overrides algorithmic confidence; integrated consensus gates ensure agreement but may miss rare atypical morphologies that the algorithm has never seen and technicians recognize intuitively. None of the options establish a learning loop—if technicians consistently override the algorithm's suggestions in one direction (e.g., rejecting high-confidence algorithm calls), how is that pattern fed back to improve the algorithm, and does it trigger protocol revision or retraining?

name: Ambient sensor placement philosophy: naturalistic density versus diagnostic completeness
consequences: Increasing infrared camera coverage, microphone density, and environmental sensor saturation improves event detection specificity and morphology characterization, but even subtle changes in perceived surveillance density—cameras at eye level versus ceiling-mounted, microphones visible versus ceiling-concealed—suppress spontaneous parasomnia expression in some participants. Conversely, minimal sensor placement risks undersampling infrequent events and forcing longer stays to achieve Aim 2 statistical power.
options: Deploy cameras, microphones, and motion sensors at full diagnostic density in bedrooms and common areas, accepting that some participants experience suppressed behavior during early stay nights until habituation occurs, with documented acclimatization curves

Restrict all cameras to hallways and stairwells only, relying on contact-free mattress sensors, dry-electrode EEG, and infrared hand/body trackers visible to participants as deliberately non-invasive, acknowledging reduced capture of nuanced motor patterns within the sleep suite

Implement layered sensor introduction—participants consent to baseline monitoring only (EEG headband, mattress sensor, ambient microphone in sleeping area) for the first 3 nights, with enhanced optical and microphone coverage activated only after confirmed event capture or as part of escalation protocols
review: The core tension is whether the facility trades participant authenticity (and recruitment willingness) against morphological fidelity. All three options assume habituation is either fast enough or acceptable as a confound, but none explicitly addresses whether early-stay behavioral suppression systematically biases first-event characterization or whether late-stay behavior truly represents the home phenotype—the facility may capture only a 'clinical' parasomnia variant.

name: Technician skill baseline and escalation authority: credentialed sleep technologist path versus expedited cross-training model
consequences: Hiring exclusively board-certified or German-credentialed sleep technicians (DGSM-recognized) ensures consistent, reliable scoring and event flagging with lower inter-technician variability, but creates a bottleneck during hiring and rotation, potentially delaying start-up or forcing expensive contract labor if retention gaps emerge. A faster cross-training pipeline—hiring neuroscience graduates or nursing students and providing intensive 6–8 week on-site training—accelerates staffing but introduces heterogeneous annotation quality and requires tighter PI oversight during early operations.
options: Recruit exclusively qualified German or European sleep medicine technicians with documented polysomnography certification (DGSM or equivalent), extending the hiring timeline by 3–4 months but ensuring consistent baseline competence and reducing real-time PI supervision burden during night operations

Hire bachelor's-level neuroscience or nursing graduates without sleep-specific credentials and deliver a structured 8-week paid on-site apprenticeship covering AASM scoring, sensor troubleshooting, safety protocols, and night-shift decision-making, paired with dual-technician mentorship for the first 3 months of independent night coverage

Adopt a hybrid model in which the core overnight rotation comprises two experienced credentialed technicians plus one apprentice per night shift, creating tiered authority where the credentialed technician retains escalation decisions and final event flagging, accepting higher labor costs but maintaining quality control while building the next generation of staff
review: The tension is between hiring speed and annotation consistency; the facility cannot move faster than its staffing pipeline, yet tight budgets constrain simultaneous hiring of multiple experienced technicians. None of the options price in the turnover risk—a single technician departure mid-trial creates a vacuum that retraining cannot fill quickly, and no option specifies what happens if a newly trained technician's quality diverges sharply from the credentialed lead during their first independent night.

name: Matched control recruitment strategy: contemporaneous household-member matching versus population-based enrollment
consequences: Enrolling sleep-normal household members (partners, adult children) of NREM parasomnia participants creates perfectly matched controls for home environment, occupational exposure, and domestic schedule, but introduces selection bias toward cohabiting participants and creates ethical complexity if household members feel obligated to participate. Enrolling unrelated population controls through regional health registries or advertisements ensures independence, but baseline nocturnal characteristics (age, sleep stage distribution, arousal frequency) may differ systematically, requiring larger control N for statistical power or introducing unmeasured confounders in the event-triage benchmarking.
options: Prioritize enrollment of sleep-normal bed partners, adult children, or household members of recruited parasomnia participants, offering the same per-night compensation (€80) and residential stay opportunities, and explicitly document any household relationship as a potential confound in the baseline characterization paper

Recruit matched controls entirely through open advertisement to the German sleep research community and regional hospital networks, screening for absence of sleep disorders and age-matching to enrolled parasomnia participants, ensuring complete independence but accepting that some controls may have comorbid conditions or occupational sleep disruption not present in household-member samples

Implement a two-tier control strategy: enroll up to 8 controls via household recruitment as the primary arm, then supplement with advertisement-based community controls if household recruitment saturates before meeting the target of 12–15 controls, allowing flexibility to maximize matching while preserving independence where feasible
review: The core tension is selection bias versus recruitment feasibility; household-member controls are more naturalistic but exclude parasomnia participants without partners or who decline to involve family, potentially skewing the participant cohort toward younger, cohabiting patients. None of the options address whether controls sleeping in an unfamiliar residential research unit produce nocturnal arousals or stage-transition patterns systematically different from home, rendering the 'baseline' comparison group unrepresentative of either true baseline or parasomnia-household cohabitation.

name: Neurophysiological phenotyping depth: focus on EEG spectral markers versus multi-modal behavioral-physiological integration
consequences: Concentrating analysis on EEG power spectra, sleep stage distribution, and arousal microstructure during parasomnia episodes yields robust, replicable neurophysiological findings aligned with existing AASM sleep medicine literature and reduces dependence on video annotation quality. However, parasomnia morphology—motor sequences, trajectory, injury risk—is captured only through video and contact-free sensors, and decoupling neuroscience from behavioral characterization limits Aim 2's ability to link neural markers to clinical phenotypes (e.g., do certain EEG patterns predict wandering versus confined-space thrashing?).
options: Establish the phenotyping analysis pipeline around EEG/PSG measures—spectral power, sleep stage transitions, cardiac-autonomic coupling via HRV, and arousal density—with video review as a secondary annotation tool to document event occurrence rather than motor morphology, streamlining statistical analysis and enabling clear neuroscience publication

Conduct dual-domain analysis in which EEG metrics are paired with frame-by-frame video and contact-free sensor motion tracking to characterize motor trajectory, limb velocity, travel distance, and collision risk per episode, requiring custom annotation frameworks and longer manual review time but generating integrated phenotypes linking neural state to clinical consequence

Adopt a staged analysis approach: phase one (months 1–12) publishes EEG-focused neurophysiology and preliminary event phenotyping; phase two (months 13–24) integrates video and sensor-derived motor metrics into secondary analyses only after EEG findings are solidified, reducing cognitive load during early manuscript development but deferring full phenotypic characterization
review: The tension is between scientific depth and publication velocity; multi-modal integration generates richer phenotypes but requires custom video annotation coding systems, longer inter-rater reliability calibration, and delays to first publication. None of the options address how to handle the gap between motion-sensor-detected events and EEG confirmation—some contact-free mattress detections may be false positives or non-parasomnia arousals, and the strategy for segregating true events from false positives across modalities remains unspecified in all three approaches.

name: Pharmaceutical partnership engagement timing: pre-launch co-design versus post-validation licensing model
consequences: Early pharmaceutical collaboration during year-one setup (defining endpoints, prioritizing biomarker discovery, drafting data-sharing agreements) accelerates algorithm development and ensures Aim 3 tools are clinically relevant to industry drug-development workflows, but introduces publication delays via the 30-day pre-publication comment window and risks subtle scope creep toward biomarker discovery that diverts resources from core methodological validation. Deferring partnership until after Aim 2 methods validation and first publication preserves academic independence and prevents premature commitment to endpoints, but risks missing a critical pharma window or losing partnership leverage after competitors publish.
options: Invite a pharmaceutical partner during months 2–3 to co-define event-triage algorithm success metrics, biomarker priorities, and data-sharing frameworks, with formal agreement specifying the 30-day review window and joint steering committee meetings quarterly, accepting publication timeline negotiation as a standard cost of partnership

Operate independently through Aim 2 completion and first publication of longitudinal parasomnia characterization, then selectively approach pharmaceutical partners with a proposal to validate the event-triage tool against their internal cohorts or support phase-two expansion, preserving full academic control until partnership terms are favorable and research priorities are established

Maintain public exploratory partnership discussions with 2–3 mid-stage pharma or biotech firms interested in parasomnia biology without formal legal agreements through months 1–18, gathering input on clinical-relevance priorities while retaining full publication autonomy until a partnership MOU is signed, trading off reduced structure for flexibility
review: The core tension is scientific independence versus industry alignment; early partnership accelerates resources but subordinates research strategy to pharma timelines and risk tolerance. None of the options specify what happens if a pharma partner funds a specific Aim 3 sub-study (e.g., biomarker discovery in RBD participants) that produces unexpectedly negative or commercially inconvenient results—the pre-publication window could become a negotiation point rather than a simple courtesy review.

name: Participant length-of-stay ceiling flexibility: hard 10-week cap versus adaptive duration tied to event-yield milestones
consequences: A strict 10-week enrollment cap ensures predictable financial accounting and participant burden and prevents indefinite stays that demoralize participants with low event rates. However, if a participant captures a parasomnia event in week 9, extending by 2 weeks to characterize recurrence patterns and test escalation protocols may yield critical Aim 2 data; conversely, a participant with zero events after 8 weeks cannot generate individual-level phenotypes regardless of extension. An adaptive duration model—conditional on event capture timing—maximizes scientific yield but introduces unpredictable per-participant costs and complicates resource allocation between concurrent admissions.
options: Implement a fixed 8-week target stay for all participants with a strict 10-week maximum irrespective of event capture, enforcing uniform enrollment logistics and budget predictability; participants with zero captured events after 8 weeks are discharged and classified as unproductive admissions, triggering recruitment-criteria review if the unproductive rate exceeds 20%

Adopt an event-contingent extension policy in which participants remain 8 weeks by default, but if any parasomnia event is captured before week 8, they are offered a 2-week extension to enable escalation-protocol testing and secondary event characterization, with written consent for the extended stay and explicit cost acknowledgment

Allow flexible stay duration negotiated at week 6 based on observed event frequency: participants with ≥2 captured events by week 6 are offered a 2-week extension; participants with exactly 1 event are offered a 1-week extension; participants with zero events are discharged after 8 weeks with the option of a separate readmission in year three if recruitment gaps justify it, balancing yield with budget flexibility
review: The core tension is between statistical completeness and operational predictability; adaptive duration maximizes rare-event capture but creates uneven participant experiences and budgeting complexity. None of the options specify what happens to participants who capture their first event in week 9 or 10—does the facility have escalation PSG capacity available, or does extending stay beyond week 8 risk reduced diagnostic fidelity due to staffing constraints or facility booking pressures?

name: Semi-automated triage model target performance: clinical safety threshold versus statistical publication threshold
consequences: Setting the Aim 3 event-triage algorithm to prioritize sensitivity (catching all probable events at the cost of false positives) ensures no true parasomnia episodes are missed during overnight monitoring, supporting real-time clinical decisions (e.g., escalation protocol deployment), but generates a high false-positive burden on the dual-human scoring team and inflates manual review effort. Optimizing for balanced F1 performance or specificity instead reduces annotation workload but risks underdetecting infrequent episodes, potentially failing the minimum event-capture threshold needed for Aim 2 analysis and undermining the 'safety' argument for residential monitoring.
options: Design the algorithm with a sensitivity target of ≥90% at the cost of false-positive rates up to 30–40%, ensuring that overnight technicians are alerted to nearly all candidate events for real-time escalation or safety assessment, then allow dual-human scorers to segregate true parasomnia from false positives in the review pipeline, treating the algorithm as a safety-first screening tool rather than a publishable diagnostic classifier

Optimize the algorithm for balanced sensitivity-specificity (F1 score ≥0.75) using a validation dataset of manually scored nights, aiming to minimize technician alert fatigue while maintaining ≥80% sensitivity for true events, then report performance on both the training cohort and hold-out test set separately to distinguish publication-ready metrics from operational safety margins

Develop two algorithm configurations: a high-sensitivity 'night-shift mode' deployed during live monitoring with a 90%+ sensitivity target for real-time alerts, paired with a high-specificity 'research-annotation mode' applied post-hoc to filtered event candidates to support publication claims, transparently documenting the model-selection criterion for each use case in methods
review: The tension is between operational safety (catching everything) and statistical precision (reporting only validated detections); high sensitivity protects participants overnight but generates publication controversy if the paper claims algorithm performance without clearly disclosing the false-positive rate or separating runtime behavior from post-hoc scoring performance. None of the options address how to handle systematic false positives—if the algorithm consistently flags voluntary movements or respiratory arousals as parasomnia events, does retraining on those specific false-positive patterns degrade sensitivity for genuine parasomnia or improve specificity without penalty?
